title: Catamaran vs Motor Yacht in Marbella: The Family Charter Guide
subtitle: A practical, skipper-side comparison of catamarans and motor yachts for a family day on the water from Puerto Banus, covering stability, deck space, speed, cost, what to pack and how the day actually runs, with real boats and real prices from our fleet.
author: BoatHire24 Guides

sections.heading: Two very different days on the same coast
sections.html:
Choosing between a catamaran and a motor yacht for a family day out in Marbella comes down to four things: space, stability, speed and budget. Both have genuine strengths, and there is no single right answer. The best fit depends on your group's size, the age range aboard, and how you actually want to spend the day. Some families want to drop the anchor once in a flat, sheltered bay and stay there for hours, swimming and lounging in the sun. Others want to cover coastline, see three or four coves, and let the teenagers feel the boat move. A catamaran suits the first kind of day, a motor yacht the second, and this guide breaks down exactly why using real boats from our fleet so you can decide with confidence rather than guesswork.

Everything here is written from the perspective of the people who run these charters week in and week out through the season. The numbers are the numbers we quote at the dock, the boats are the boats on our pontoon at Puerto Banus, and the advice is what we tell families when they call and ask which way to go. Whichever you choose, both options launch from the same marina, both come with a licensed Spanish skipper, and both include fuel, drinks, snacks and VAT in the price you book, so the comparison is genuinely like for like. The decision is about the character of your day, not about hidden costs or surprises at the end of it.

sections.heading: Stability and comfort: why catamarans win with young kids
sections.html:
Catamarans have two hulls, which means they roll far less than a single-hull motor yacht. The Lagoon 380, at 11.6 metres and rated for 10 guests, is the clearest example in our fleet: its wide beam creates a stable platform even in moderate chop, and the twin-hull design reduces rolling by up to 80% compared with a monohull. For families with toddlers or grandparents aboard, that difference is enormous. Seasickness is genuinely rare on a cat, and a child who would turn green on a rolling deck will often happily sit and eat lunch on a catamaran without noticing it is moving at all. If anyone in your group is prone to motion sickness, this is the single most important factor in the whole comparison.

Motor yachts are comfortable too. The Azimut 39, a 12.5 metre flybridge boat for 11 guests, sits well in the water and, especially with stabilisers, gives a smooth ride. But a monohull still has a gentle roll that some people notice, particularly at anchor when the boat sits beam-on to a passing wake or a light swell. That roll is part of the character of a motor yacht and most adults barely register it, but for the very young and the very old it can be the difference between a relaxed afternoon and an uncomfortable one. If your absolute priority is flat-water comfort, the catamaran wins hands down. If your family is made up of confident swimmers and teenagers who actively enjoy a bit of movement, the roll of a motor yacht is a non-issue and you can weight the decision towards the things motor yachts do better.
sections.image_caption: The Lagoon 380 catamaran, 11.6 metres and rated for 10 guests, the steadiest platform in the fleet for families with young children.

sections.heading: Deck space and layout: catamarans offer more room per metre
sections.html:
A catamaran's twin hulls allow for a much larger usable deck area than a monohull of the same length. The Lagoon 380 has a spacious aft cockpit, a saloon that opens fully to the stern so inside and outside become one connected space, and a trampoline net at the bow for lounging. That works out to roughly 40% more usable deck space than a motor yacht of the same length, which is a striking difference when you have a family spread out across the boat doing different things. Children can be on the bow trampoline while the adults sit in the shaded cockpit and someone else swims off the back, and nobody is on top of anybody else. For a family that wants room to breathe, the catamaran is the better choice almost by definition.

Motor yachts use their space differently. The Astondoa 40, a 12.5 metre motor yacht for 9 guests, has a more compact layout with a sunpad forward and a smaller aft deck, which is efficient and sociable but does not give you the same sprawling lounging area. Where motor yachts pull ahead is in the vertical dimension. Boats with flybridges, such as the Azimut 58 Flybridge at 17.6 metres and 12 guests, add an entire upper deck with panoramic views and a second sunbathing and seating area that kids genuinely love. A flybridge gives you a high vantage point to spot dolphins, watch the coast slide by, and feel like you are on top of the world, and that is something a catamaran cannot match. So the honest summary is this: catamarans give you more flat space to spread out, motor yachts with flybridges give you a second level and a better view.

BoatTypeLengthGuestsStandout space feature
Lagoon 380Catamaran11.6 m10Bow trampoline plus wide cockpit
Astondoa 40Motor yacht12.5 m9Forward sunpad
Azimut 39Motor yacht12.5 m11Flybridge plus convertible saloon
Azimut 58 FlybridgeMotor yacht17.6 m12Full upper deck with panoramic views

sections.heading: Speed and range: motor yachts cover more coastline
sections.html:
If you want to visit multiple coves in a single day, speed matters more than almost anything else, and this is the area where motor yachts are simply in a different league. The Astondoa 40 cruises at 22 to 25 knots and reaches Cala del Faro from Puerto Banus in about 12 minutes. The Mangusta 80, a 24 metre boat for 12 guests, can hit more than 30 knots. Catamarans are slower by nature: the Lagoon 380 cruises at 8 to 10 knots. To put that in concrete terms, a trip from Puerto Banus to Cabopino takes about 20 minutes on a motor yacht versus roughly 40 minutes on a catamaran. The same gap shows up everywhere along the coast: reaching Cala Cortes from Puerto Banus takes the Astondoa 40 about 15 minutes, but the Lagoon 380 closer to 30.

That difference compounds across a day. If your family wants to see Estepona, Sotogrande, or the cliffs of Cala Cortes in a half-day charter, a motor yacht is far more practical, because the time you save on each transit is time you get back in the water. On a catamaran, the slower cruise is part of the appeal rather than a problem, but it does mean you plan a different kind of day: pick one good bay, settle in, and treat the gentle pace of getting there as part of the relaxation rather than a means to an end. The rule of thumb we give families is simple. If your day is about covering ground and seeing variety, choose a motor yacht. If your day is about anchoring once and staying put, the catamaran's slower speed costs you nothing because you were not planning to move much anyway.
sections.image_caption: The Astondoa 40 cruises at 22 to 25 knots and reaches Cala del Faro from Puerto Banus in about 12 minutes.

sections.heading: Price comparison: what you get for your money
sections.html:
Our Tier A motor yachts, the Astondoa 40 and the Azimut 39, cost €749 for 2 hours and €2,299 for 8 hours, and that price includes the skipper, fuel, drinks, snacks and VAT. There are no hidden extras and no bar bill at the end of the day. The Lagoon 380 catamaran is priced on request, but you should expect a premium of roughly 15 to 25% over a similar-sized motor yacht, because the larger deck area and the higher demand for catamarans push the rate up. For a full-day family charter of 8 hours, the realistic budget to plan around is €2,300 to €3,500 depending on the boat. Both options include all the essentials, so whichever way you go, the number you are quoted is the number you pay.

It is worth understanding why the catamaran carries a premium, because it is not arbitrary. You are paying for that extra 40% of deck space, for the stability that keeps the youngest and oldest members of the family comfortable, and for a boat that is in genuinely high demand precisely because families love it. The motor yacht, for the same or slightly less money, gives you speed and range instead. Neither is better value in the abstract; they are different products at a similar price point. The table below lays out the headline numbers so you can see the comparison at a glance, and the practical takeaway is that for most families the deciding factor will not be the few hundred euros between them but the kind of day each boat delivers.

sections.heading: Activities and extras: what families can do onboard
sections.html:
Both boat types open up the same core activities: swimming, snorkelling and paddleboarding straight off the boat. Where they differ is in the detail of how easy each is for children. The Lagoon 380's low, wide swim platform makes it genuinely easy for kids to get in and out of the water, which matters more than it sounds when you have done it twenty times in an afternoon with a five-year-old. Motor yachts have generous platforms too, and the Azimut 58 Flybridge in particular has a spacious bathing platform that works well for a family. We include snorkel gear on all charters, so there is nothing extra to hire or carry down to the pontoon.

For families who want a particular activity, the extras are where you can tailor the day. We can add a jet ski to any charter, and the Mangusta 80 includes one free, which turns a charter into something that keeps energetic teenagers entertained for hours. Family-friendly extras like paddleboards and jet skis can be added to most charters, so you are not locked into a fixed package. The match between activity and boat is fairly intuitive: for families who want to anchor in a quiet bay like Cala Cortes and spend hours simply swimming and lounging, the catamaran's stability turns the boat itself into a floating beach. For thrill-seeking teens who want speed and a jet ski, a motor yacht is the more exciting base for the day. Think about what your group actually wants to do in the water before you choose the hull, because the right boat makes those activities easier rather than just possible.
sections.image_caption: The Azimut 58 Flybridge, 17.6 metres for 12 guests, with a spacious swim platform and an upper deck kids love.

sections.heading: Weather and season: matching the hull to the conditions
sections.html:
The conditions you are likely to meet should feed directly into your choice of boat, and the season makes a real difference here. In Marbella's summer, from June to September, sea conditions are generally calm, so both boat types perform well and the stability gap matters less. In the shoulder months, April and May at the start of the season and October at the end, the wind can pick up, and this is where the catamaran earns its reputation. A cat handles choppy water far better thanks to its inherent stability, staying flat and comfortable when a monohull would start to move. Motor yachts with deep-V hulls, such as the Pershing 46, are built to cut through a swell but can feel rougher in one, so a windy shoulder-season day is felt more aboard a motor yacht than aboard a catamaran.

The practical advice that follows is straightforward. For a high-summer family trip in July or August, when the water is reliably calm, either boat will give you a smooth day and you can choose purely on space and speed. For a spring or autumn family trip in the shoulder months, when the breeze is less predictable, a catamaran is the safer bet for comfort, especially with young children aboard who will not enjoy a bouncy ride. Your skipper reads the morning forecast before you cast off and plans the day around it whichever boat you are on, choosing sheltered water when the wind is up, but the hull underneath you sets the baseline for how the conditions feel, and a catamaran simply gives you more margin on a marginal day.

sections.heading: Which boat is best for your family?
sections.html:
Pulling the threads together, the decision usually resolves cleanly once you know what kind of day you want. Choose a catamaran, the Lagoon 380, if you have young children or elderly family members aboard, if flat-water stability is your top priority, and if you want maximum deck space for lounging and spreading out. It is the boat for the family that wants to find one beautiful bay, drop the anchor, and stay there. Choose a motor yacht, the Astondoa 40, the Azimut 39, or the Azimut 58 Flybridge, if you want the speed to visit multiple spots in a day, if you have teenagers who enjoy faster rides and a bit of thrill, or if you prefer a flybridge for the views and the second deck. Both are excellent for families, and the decision genuinely comes down to your pace and your priorities rather than one boat being better than the other.

Group size and age mix tend to push the choice one way or the other. The Lagoon 380 holds up to 10 guests, which suits most family groups comfortably. Our motor yachts range from 8 guests on the Bandido up to 12 on the Mangusta 80 and the Ferretti 94, so for larger family parties a motor yacht like the Azimut 58 Flybridge is ideal when you need to seat 12. For a family with teenagers specifically, it is worth knowing that teenagers often prefer the speed and thrill of a motor yacht, and the Pershing 46, at 14 metres for 10 guests, is sporty and fun in a way that lands well with that age group. For a more relaxed vibe with space to spread out and play, the Lagoon 380 offers trampolines and a stable platform for games. Both work; it really does depend on your family's energy level on the day.

sections.heading: What to bring and how to prepare
sections.html:
Whichever boat you choose, a family charter here is deliberately easy to pack for, because the boat carries most of what you need. We supply snorkel gear on every charter, and there is a coolbox and fridge on board stocked with drinks. What is genuinely worth bringing yourself comes down to a short, sensible list, and getting it right makes the day run more smoothly, especially with children who feel the sun and the wind more than adults do.

  • High-factor sun cream and hats for everyone, because the reflection off the water doubles the exposure and children burn quickly.
  • A light layer each for the run home, when the apparent wind on a fast motor yacht can feel cool even on a hot day.
  • Soft-soled shoes or none at all to keep the decks clean and safe; everything aboard works better barefoot.
  • Your own food and additional drinks if you want them. You are welcome to bring extra on board, and there is room in the fridge and coolbox.
  • Swimwear under your clothes so the children can be in the water within minutes of the anchor going down rather than queuing to change.

One important safety note that applies to both boat types: avoid glass bottles. If you bring your own drinks, decant them or choose cans, because broken glass on a deck full of bare feet is the one thing that can spoil a family day. Beyond that, the preparation is light. The skipper handles everything technical, the drinks are already aboard, and the gear is provided, so your job is really just sun protection, swimwear and a relaxed attitude to the timings.

sections.heading: Skipper tips and how the day actually runs
sections.html:
A family charter follows a simple, predictable rhythm whichever hull you choose. You board at Puerto Banus, the skipper runs a short safety brief and reads the forecast with you, and then you head out towards whichever stretch of coast suits the conditions and the kind of day you have chosen. On a fast motor yacht you might be anchored in a swimmable bay within fifteen minutes; on the catamaran the run is more leisurely and the boat itself is part of the experience from the moment you leave the marina. The anchor goes down on sand, the skipper sets it properly, and then the time is yours to swim, snorkel, paddleboard or simply sit on the bathing platform with a drink while the children wear themselves out in the water.

A few pieces of practical advice make a family day better, and they come straight from the people who run these boats. First, take the earliest slot you can, because the water is calmest in the morning before the afternoon sea breeze builds, and calm water is easier for nervous swimmers and more comfortable for anyone sensitive to movement. Second, plan around the youngest member of the group rather than the oldest, because the day succeeds or fails on whether the small children are happy, and that means a stable boat, an easy swim platform and a bay sheltered from the chop. Third, do not over-pack the itinerary. One or two good stops with real time in the water beat a rushed tour of five coves, particularly on a catamaran where the slower cruise makes a multi-stop plan unrealistic anyway. The skipper stays aboard the entire time you are in the water, watching the boat and the swimmers, so there is always a responsible adult on the boat while the family plays, and that peace of mind is part of what you are paying for.
sections.image_caption: The Azimut 39, a 12.5 metre flybridge motor yacht for 11 guests, a popular Tier A choice for a full family day.

sections.heading: Nearby alternatives and what is included
sections.html:
If neither the standard catamaran nor the Tier A motor yacht is quite the right fit, the fleet has alternatives that cover the edges. For a larger family party that needs the full 12 seats with a sporty edge, the Mangusta 80 brings more than 30 knots of speed and a free jet ski, which is the option for a group that wants both space and excitement. For families who want the absolute top of the range, boats like the Ferretti 94 carry up to 12 guests in serious comfort. For a smaller, more intimate family group, the Bandido seats up to 8. The point is that the catamaran-versus-motor-yacht choice is the starting framework, not the whole menu, and once you know which direction suits your family there is usually a boat that fits the exact size and budget you have in mind.

Every charter, on every boat, comes with the same honest, all-inclusive package so the comparison stays clean and there is never a surprise at the end:

  • A licensed Spanish skipper who knows this coast and stays aboard throughout
  • Fuel for the whole charter, with no surcharge
  • Water, soft drinks, beer, white wine and cava on ice, plus light snacks
  • Snorkel gear on every charter, with paddleboards and jet skis available as extras
  • Full insurance and safety equipment, and Spanish VAT at the standard rate

Booking is simple. Pick a date, tell us your group size and the ages aboard, and say whether you want a relaxed day at anchor or a faster tour of the coast, and we will match you to the right boat. For most families the easiest way to try the water is a 2-hour Tier A slot at €749, while the 8-hour day at €2,299 on a motor yacht, or the equivalent full day on the Lagoon 380, is the one that turns a charter into a proper family occasion. Departures are from Puerto Banus, which puts the best of the coast, from Cala del Faro to Cabopino and Cala Cortes, within easy reach whichever hull you step aboard.
